skill.name: tool-discovery
skill.description: Helps users choose the right Claude, Codex, or Claude Code skill or agent for a task using curated catalogs and selection rules.
skill.summary: Choose the right agent or skill for each development task.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high raw score because it treated markdown code spans and catalog prose as executable patterns. Manual review found documentation-only content with no scripts, credential access, outbound network endpoint, prompt injection, or malicious intent. The only retained concern is generic shell and package-install guidance that users should review before running.

low_findings.title: Generic Command Guidance in Markdown Reference
low_findings.description: The reference includes generic shell commands for checking package and CLI availability, plus optional package installation examples. This is a low-risk operational concern because the skill is documentation-only, has no executable scripts, and does not include user-controlled command construction.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line-numbered context shows literal bash examples, not executable skill code. The commands are generic verification and install guidance without exfiltration or injection behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positives in Markdown Catalogs
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but the cited lines are markdown tables, tool names, catalog entries, or ordinary words. No evidence found of dynamic execution, weak cryptographic implementation, credential access, or malicious network use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files contain reference prose and markdown code spans, not source code that runs commands or cryptography. Multiple high-risk hits resolve to words such as design, codes, or catalog descriptions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generic Command Guidance in Markdown Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference includes generic shell commands for checking package and CLI availability, plus optional package installation examples. This is a low-risk operational concern because the skill is documentation-only, has no executable scripts, and does not include user-controlled command construction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line-numbered context shows literal bash examples, not executable skill code. The commands are generic verification and install guidance without exfiltration or injection behavior.

content.user_title: Choose the Right Claude and Codex Tool
content.value_statement: Teams lose time deciding whether a task needs a skill, an agent, or manual work. This skill provides a compact routing guide for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use lightweight skills versus heavyweight agents.
Lists common skills with trigger phrases and matching tasks.
Lists common agents with capabilities and best-use cases.
Maps task examples to first-choice skills and backup agents.
Provides reference catalogs for file formats, CLI tools, and domains.
limitations: Does not install, launch, or configure agents or skills automatically.
Catalog accuracy depends on the available tools in the user environment.
Does not verify whether recommended third-party packages are installed.
Not a security approval system for unknown agents or skills.

use_cases.title: Select a Tool for a Coding Task
use_cases.description: Choose whether a development request should use a reference skill, an expert agent, or a general planning workflow.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Agent Routing
use_cases.description: Give team members a shared guide for matching recurring requests to approved Claude Code agents and skills.

use_cases.title: Find File and Data Processing Helpers
use_cases.description: Identify practical tools for documents, archives, structured data, web tasks, and command-line workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a Tool
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection for a single task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tool-discovery to recommend the best skill or agent for this task: [describe the task]. Explain the reason briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose between skill and agent paths.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tool-discovery to compare the best skill and agent options for: [task]. Include when each option is appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Workflow Route
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan tool selection across several related tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tool-discovery to map these tasks to skills or agents: [task list]. Keep the recommendations practical and ordered.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Tool Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of an existing agent or skill plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tool-discovery to review this proposed tool plan: [plan]. Identify mismatches, missing tools, and simpler alternatives.

output_examples.input: I need to find every TypeScript file that calls a deprecated function.
output_examples.output: Use file-search first for broad content search. Use structural-search if you need syntax-aware matching across function calls.

output_examples.input: I need help deciding how to optimize a slow PostgreSQL query.
output_examples.output: Use postgres-expert because the task requires query reasoning, execution plan review, and index strategy decisions.

output_examples.input: I need to transform a JSON configuration file and keep the process repeatable.
output_examples.output: Use data-processing for jq or yq patterns. Add an expert agent only if the transformation needs design decisions.

best_practices: Start with a skill for lookup tasks and known workflows.
Use an agent when the task needs judgment, architecture, or optimization.
Limit recommendations to two or three tools so the user can act quickly.
anti_patterns: Do not use an expert agent for a simple syntax lookup.
Do not follow catalog commands without checking local project policy.
Do not assume every listed tool exists in the current environment.

faq.question: Does this skill run tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides selection guidance and reference catalogs. The user or assistant still chooses what to run.

faq.question: When should I choose a skill instead of an agent?
faq.answer: Choose a skill for quick references, syntax patterns, file searches, and known repeatable workflows.

faq.question: When should I choose an agent?
faq.answer: Choose an agent when the task needs reasoning, architecture choices, debugging judgment, or performance decisions.

faq.question: Can it help with Claude Code workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Claude Code oriented guidance for Task tool agents and Skill tool references.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive commands?
faq.answer: It includes generic command examples for checking tools and packages. Review commands before using them.

faq.question: Is this a complete catalog of every possible tool?
faq.answer: No. It is a curated guide for the tools listed in its local reference files.
